processors:
  - set:
      field: ecs.version
      value: 8.11.0
  - set:
      field: event.kind
      value: event
  - set:
      field: event.module
      value: mysql
  - set:
      field: event.category
      value: ["database"]
  - set:
      field: event.type
      value: ["info"]
  - set:
      field: user.name
      value: '{{sql.metrics.string.source_user}}'
      ignore_empty_value: true
  - set:
      field: service.type
      value: "mysql"
  - set:
      if: ctx.sql?.metrics?.numeric?.auto_position == 1
      field: sql.metrics.numeric.auto_position
      value: true
      ignore_empty_value: true
  - set:
      if: ctx.sql?.metrics?.numeric?.auto_position == 0
      field: sql.metrics.numeric.auto_position
      value: false
      ignore_empty_value: true
  - set:
      if: ctx.sql?.metrics?.numeric?.get_source_public_key == 1
      field: sql.metrics.numeric.get_source_public_key
      value: true

input: What is the description of the field 'cloud.instance.name', and how does it differ from 'cloud.instance.id'?
actual_output:
The field **`cloud.instance.id`** is a unique identifier for an instance, typically sourced from cloud provider-specific labels (e.g., GCP/azure.instanceId). It is system-generated and immutable. 

The field **`cloud.instance.name`** represents the instance's human-readable name, often derived from labels like GCP/azure.instanceName or cleaned/renamed fields. It may be user-defined or follow naming conventions. 

**Key difference**: `cloud.instance.id` is a unique, system-assigned ID, while `cloud.instance.name` is a descriptive label.
expected_output: The field 'cloud.instance.name' refers to the instance name of the host machine, which is a human-readable name assigned to the cloud instance. This differs from 'cloud.instance.id', which is a unique identifier assigned to the cloud instance. While 'cloud.instance.id' is typically a system-generated string that uniquely identifies the instance across all instances, 'cloud.instance.name' can be set by the user to something more descriptive or meaningful, aiding in easier identification and management of the instance.

title: "GCP Dataproc Metrics"
type: metrics
streams:
  - input: gcp/metrics
    title: GCP Dataproc Metrics
    description: Collect GCP Dataproc Metrics
    vars:
      - name: region
        type: text
        title: GCP Region
        description: deprecated, use regions instead
        multi: false
        required: false
        show_user: true
      - name: regions
        type: text
        title: GCP Regions
        description: A list of GCP regions to pull data from
        multi: true
        required: false
        show_user: true
      - name: period
        type: text
        title: Period
        default: 60s
        required: true
      - name: exclude_labels
        type: bool
        title: Exclude Labels
        description: Exclude additional labels from metrics
        multi: false
        required: false
        show_user: true
elasticsearch:
  index_mode: "time_series"

- name: gcp.dataproc
  description: Google Cloud Dataproc metrics
  type: group
  fields:
    - name: batch.spark.executors.count
      type: long
      metric_type: gauge
      description: Indicates the number of Batch Spark executors.
    - name: cluster.hdfs.datanodes.count
      type: long
      metric_type: gauge
      description: Indicates the number of HDFS DataNodes that are running inside a cluster.
    - name: cluster.hdfs.storage_capacity.value
      type: double
      metric_type: gauge
      description: Indicates capacity of HDFS system running on cluster in GB.
    - name: cluster.hdfs.storage_utilization.value
      type: double
      metric_type: gauge
      description: The percentage of HDFS storage currently used.
    - name: cluster.hdfs.unhealthy_blocks.count
      type: long
      metric_type: gauge
      description: Indicates the number of unhealthy blocks inside the cluster.
    - name: cluster.job.failed.count
      type: long
      metric_type: gauge

### Compute

The `compute` dataset is designed to fetch metrics for [Compute Engine](https://cloud.google.com/compute/) Virtual Machines in Google Cloud Platform.

**ECS Field Reference**

Please refer to the following [document](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/ecs/current/ecs-field-reference.html) for detailed information on ECS fields.

{{fields "compute"}}

{{event "compute"}}

### Dataproc

The `dataproc` dataset is designed to fetch metrics from [Dataproc](https://cloud.google.com/dataproc/) in Google Cloud Platform.

**ECS Field Reference**

Please refer to the following [document](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/ecs/current/ecs-field-reference.html) for detailed information on ECS fields.

{{fields "dataproc"}}

{{event "dataproc"}}

### Firestore

The `firestore` dataset fetches metrics from [Firestore](https://cloud.google.com/firestore/) in Google Cloud Platform.

**ECS Field Reference**

This indicates the amount of memory that has been requested but not yet allocated to running applications or tasks.

input: Given the context provided, how would you describe the field 'gcp.firestore.document.write.count' and explain its relationship with other similar fields in the same context?
actual_output: The field `gcp.firestore.document.write.count` is a renamed version of the original `gcp.metrics.document.write.count` field, part of a pipeline that standardizes GCP Firestore metric naming. It is grouped with similar fields like `gcp.firestore.document.read.count` and `gcp.firestore.document.delete.count`, all tracking document operations in Firestore. These fields replace the older `gcp.metrics` namespace, aligning with ECS standards to provide consistent, structured metrics for monitoring Firestore activity.
expected_output: The field 'gcp.firestore.document.write.count' represents the delta of the number of successful document writes to Google Cloud Firestore. It is of type 'long' and categorized as a 'gauge' metric type, indicating that it measures the rate of change over time. This field is part of a set of related metrics that track different operations performed on Firestore documents. Similar fields include 'gcp.firestore.document.delete.count', which tracks the number of successful document deletions, and 'gcp.firestore.document.read.count', which tracks the number of successful document reads. All these fields provide insights into the usage patterns and performance of Firestore by quantifying the number of write, delete, and read operations, respectively.

### Delta Sync, Import, Security and GSI views

This is the `miscellaneous` data stream.

The Delta Sync provides the ability to replicate only those parts of a Couchbase Mobile document that have changed. 

The import is processed with an admin user context in the Sync Function, similar to writes made through the Sync Gateway Admin API.

The Security metrics give the metrics related to authentication requests such as number of authentication failures and number of access errors.

Global Secondary Indexes (GSI) support queries made by the Query Service.

input: What does the field 'gcp.destination.instance.region' represent in the context of Google Cloud Platform, and how does it relate to other location-related fields such as 'gcp.destination.instance.zone' and 'cloud.region'?
actual_output: The field **`gcp.destination.instance.region`** represents the **geographic region** (e.g., "us-central1") where a Google Cloud VM is located. It is mapped to **`cloud.region`** in data streams for consistency. The **`gcp.destination.instance.zone`** field specifies a more granular **availability zone** within that region (e.g., "us-central1-a"). Both are used to describe the VM's location, with `region` being broader than `zone`. The `cloud.region` field is a standardized version of the VM's region, derived from `gcp.destination.instance.region`.
expected_output: The field 'gcp.destination.instance.region' represents the region where the destination VM (Virtual Machine) is located within Google Cloud Platform. It indicates the broader geographical area where the VM is hosted. This field is related to 'gcp.destination.instance.zone', which specifies the more precise location within the region, as zones are distinct fault domains within a region. Additionally, 'cloud.region' serves a similar purpose but at a higher level of abstraction, potentially encompassing multiple resources and not limited to VM instances. In summary, while 'cloud.region' can refer to any resource's region, 'gcp.destination.instance.region' specifically pertains to the region of the destination VM, and 'gcp.destination.instance.zone' further narrows down the location to a specific zone within that region.

    title: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) firewall logs (gcp-pubsub)
    description: Collect Google Cloud Platform (GCP) firewall logs using gcp-pubsub input

suspicious changes in network configurations.

### Possible investigation steps

- Review the audit logs for entries with the event.dataset field set to gcp.audit to confirm the source of the alert.
- Examine the event.action field for values such as *.compute.firewalls.patch or google.appengine.*.Firewall.Update*Rule to identify the specific type of firewall rule modification.
- Identify the user or service account responsible for the modification by checking the actor information in the audit logs.
- Assess the changes made to the firewall rule, including the before and after states, to determine if the modification allows more permissive ingress or egress traffic.
- Investigate the context of the modification by reviewing related activities in the audit logs around the same time to identify any suspicious patterns or sequences of actions.
- Check for any recent security incidents or alerts involving the affected VPC or App Engine application to understand potential motives

# Google Cloud Platform Integration

The Google Cloud integration collects and parses Google Cloud [Audit Logs](https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/audit), [VPC Flow Logs](https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/using-flow-logs), [Firewall Rules Logs](https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/firewall-rules-logging) and [Cloud DNS Logs](https://cloud.google.com/dns/docs/monitoring) that have been exported from Cloud Logging to a Google Pub/Sub topic sink and collects Google Cloud [metrics](https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/api/metrics_gcp) and metadata from Google Cloud [Monitoring](https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/docs).

## Authentication

To use this Google Cloud Platform (GCP) integration, you need to set up a
*Service Account* with a *Role* and a *Service Account Key* to access data on
your GCP project.

### Service Account

input: What does the metric gcp.gke.node.memory.allocatable_utilization.pct specifically represent in terms of memory usage on a GKE node?
actual_output: The metric `gcp.gke.node.memory.allocatable_utilization.pct` represents the percentage of **allocatable memory** (memory available for containers, excluding system-reserved memory) that is currently in use on a GKE node. However, this specific metric is not detailed in the provided contexts.
expected_output: The metric gcp.gke.node.memory.allocatable_utilization.pct specifically represents the fraction of the allocatable memory that is currently in use on the GKE node. It indicates how much of the memory that has been designated as usable (allocatable) by Kubernetes is actually being utilized by processes and applications running on the node. This value is expressed as a percentage and is sampled every 60 seconds. Importantly, this utilization percentage cannot exceed 1 (or 100%), as the used memory cannot surpass the allocatable memory.

input: What does the metric gcp.gke.node.memory.allocatable_utilization.pct represent, and how does it relate to the allocatable memory on the node?
actual_output: The metric `gcp.gke.node.memory.allocatable_utilization.pct` represents the percentage of allocatable memory currently in use on the node. It is calculated as `(used memory / allocatable memory) * 100`, where allocatable memory is the total memory available on the node (`gcp.gke.node.memory.total.bytes`). This metric indicates how full the node's memory is relative to its available capacity.
expected_output: The metric gcp.gke.node.memory.allocatable_utilization.pct represents the fraction of the allocatable memory that is currently in use on the node. It indicates how much of the total allocatable memory (gcp.gke.node.memory.allocatable.bytes) is being utilized by the node. This value is expressed as a percentage and cannot exceed 1, meaning that the usage cannot surpass the total allocatable memory available on the node.

input: Given the context provided, how would you interpret the metric gcp.loadbalancing_metrics.https.backend_latencies.value in relation to the overall performance monitoring of an HTTPS load balancer, and what steps would you take to analyze this metric effectively?
actual_output:
The metric `gcp.loadbalancing_metrics.https.backend_latencies.value` measures the distribution of latency between the load balancer and backend servers for HTTPS requests. High values indicate potential bottlenecks, such as network delays or slow backend processing.  

**Steps to analyze effectively:**  
1. **Baseline Comparison**: Compare latencies to historical baselines to identify anomalies.  
2. **Correlate with Errors**: Check if spikes align with backend connection errors (e.g., `aws.elb.metrics.BackendConnectionErrors.sum`).  
3. **Distribution Analysis**: Examine latency percentiles (e.g., 95th, 99th) to detect outliers or slow requests.  
4. **Backend Health**: Investigate backend server performance (e.g., CPU, memory, or database load via Performance Insights).  
5. **Network Diagnostics**: Validate network connectivity and bandwidth between the load balancer and backends.  
6. **Optimize**: Address bottlenecks by scaling backends, optimizing queries, or adjusting load balancer configurations.
expected_output:
The metric gcp.loadbalancing_metrics.https.backend_latencies.value represents a distribution of the latency experienced by requests as they travel from the HTTPS load balancer to the backend servers and back. This latency is measured from the moment the request is sent by the load balancer to the backend until the load balancer receives the last byte of the response from the backend. It provides insights into the performance of the backend services and the network connectivity between the load balancer and the backends.

To analyze this metric effectively:

1. **Understand the Distribution**: Examine the distribution of latencies to identify common latency values, outliers, and any patterns that may indicate issues with specific backends or network segments.

2. **Set Baselines**: Establish baseline latency levels under normal conditions to help identify when performance degrades. Compare current latency distributions against these baselines to detect anomalies.

3. **Monitor Trends Over Time**: Track changes in latency over time to identify trends that might suggest underlying issues, such as increased load on backends or network congestion.

4. **Correlate with Other Metrics**: Compare backend latency with other relevant metrics, such as the number of requests served by backends (gcp.loadbalancing_metrics.https.backend_request.count) and the number of bytes sent as requests and responses (gcp.loadbalancing_metrics.https.backend_request.bytes, gcp.loadbalancing_metrics.https.backend_response.bytes), to understand the impact of traffic volume on latency.

5. **Investigate Anomalies**: When high latency is detected, investigate potential causes such as backend server performance issues, network bottlenecks, or configuration problems in the load balancer or backend services.

6. **Implement Alerts**: Set up alerts for significant increases in latency to proactively address performance issues before they affect end-users.

7. **Optimize Backend Performance**: Use insights gained from latency analysis to optimize backend server performance, adjust resource allocation, and improve network configurations to reduce latency and enhance overall system performance.

retrieval_context:
Amazon RDS Performance Insights enables you to monitor and explore different dimensions of database load based on data captured from a running DB instance. The guide provides detailed information about Performance Insights data types, parameters and errors.

When Performance Insights is enabled, the Amazon RDS Performance Insights API provides visibility into the performance of your DB instance. Amazon CloudWatch provides the authoritative source for Amazon Web Services service-vended monitoring metrics. Performance Insights offers a domain-specific view of DB load.

DB load is measured as average active sessions. Performance Insights provides the data to API consumers as a two-dimensional time-series dataset. The time dimension provides DB load data for each time point in the queried time range. Each time point decomposes overall load in relation to the requested dimensions, measured at that time point. Examples include SQL, Wait event, User, and Host.

For step-by-step instructions on how to set up an integration, see the
{{ url "getting-started-observability" "Getting started" }} guide.

To send classic ELB access logs to an S3 bucket, see [enable access logs for classic load balancer](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/classic/enable-access-logs.html).

For an application load balancer, see [enable access log for application load balancer](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/enable-access-logging.html) and [enable connection log for application load balancer](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/enable-connection-logging.html).

For a network load balancer, see [enable access log for network load balancer](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest//network/load-balancer-access-logs.html).

### Advanced options

#### CloudWatch

The CloudWatch logs input has several advanced options to fit specific use cases.

##### Latency

input: What does the metric gcp.redis.replication.master.slaves.lag.sec represent, and how does it relate to the replication process in Google Cloud Redis?
actual_output: The metric **gcp.redis.replication.master.slaves.lag.sec** represents the **time delay (in seconds)** between the Redis master and its slaves during replication. It measures how far behind slaves are in processing data from the master. A higher value indicates potential replication bottlenecks, such as network latency or resource constraints on slaves, which could affect data consistency and availability in Google Cloud Redis.
expected_output: The metric gcp.redis.replication.master.slaves.lag.sec represents the number of seconds that a replica is lagging behind the primary node in the replication process in Google Cloud Redis. This metric provides insight into the delay between the data being written to the primary node and the same data being available on the replica nodes. A higher value indicates a longer delay, which could be due to network latency, high write load on the primary, or other factors affecting the replication speed.

input: What does the gcp.redis.stats.cache_hit_ratio metric specifically represent in terms of its value and how is it calculated based on the provided context?
actual_output: The metric **gcp.redis.stats.cache_hit_ratio** is not mentioned or defined in the provided contexts. The available information focuses on CVSS scores, vulnerability metrics, and Google SCC findings, but does not describe this specific Redis metric.
expected_output: The gcp.redis.stats.cache_hit_ratio metric represents the cache hit ratio as a fraction. It indicates the proportion of successful key lookups (hits) compared to the total number of key lookups (hits + misses). Based on the provided context, this ratio is calculated by dividing the number of successful lookups (gcp.redis.stats.keyspace_hits.count) by the sum of successful lookups and failed lookups (gcp.redis.stats.keyspace_misses.count). The value of this metric ranges from 0 to 1, where 1 means all key lookups were successful (no misses), and 0 means no key lookups were successful (all misses).

input: What is the specific description and purpose of the 'cloud.account.id' field in a multi-tenant cloud environment?
actual_output: The `cloud.account.id` field uniquely identifies a cloud account or organization in a multi-tenant environment, enabling differentiation between entities sharing the same infrastructure. Examples include AWS account IDs, Google Cloud ORG IDs, or other unique identifiers. Its purpose is to track and isolate resources, data, and activities specific to each tenant.
expected_output: The 'cloud.account.id' field is used to identify different entities in a multi-tenant environment by specifying the cloud account or organization ID. This unique identifier helps distinguish between various accounts or organizations within the same cloud service provider, such as AWS account ID, Google Cloud ORG ID, or any other unique identifier.

input: What does azure.resource.tags.* represent in the context of Azure resources, and how does it differ from other attributes like azure.resource.name or azure.resource.type?
actual_output: `azure.resource.tags.*` represents user-defined key-value pairs used to categorize and label Azure resources, providing flexible metadata. Unlike `azure.resource.name` (a unique identifier) or `azure.resource.type` (a predefined resource category), tags are optional, customizable, and used for organizational purposes like cost tracking or access control. Tags are structured as an object or flattened fields, while name and type are single keyword fields.
expected_output: azure.resource.tags.* represents Azure resource tags, which are key-value pairs that can be added to Azure resources for organizing and managing them more effectively. These tags do not have a predefined structure and can be customized based on the user's needs. Unlike azure.resource.name, which is a specific attribute that holds the name of the resource, and azure.resource.type, which specifies the type of the resource (e.g., Virtual Machine, Storage Account), azure.resource.tags.* allows for additional metadata to be associated with the resource. This metadata can include information such as cost center, environment (development, testing, production), or any other relevant details that help in categorizing and filtering resources within Azure.

input: What specific information does 'host.domain' provide about the host's membership in a domain, and how does it differ between Windows and Linux environments?
actual_output: The `host.domain` field provides the domain name of which the host is a member. On **Windows**, this typically refers to the Active Directory domain or NetBIOS domain name. On **Linux**, it refers to the domain of the host's LDAP provider. The difference lies in the domain service infrastructure used by each OS.
expected_output: The 'host.domain' field provides the name of the domain to which the host belongs. In a Windows environment, this could refer to the host's Active Directory domain or its NetBIOS domain name. On the other hand, in a Linux environment, it typically refers to the domain associated with the host's LDAP provider.

input: Given the context provided, how would you interpret the metric 'azure.compute_vm_scaleset.outbound_flows_maximum_creation_rate.avg' in relation to the overall network performance and potential scaling needs of a virtual machine scale set?
actual_output: The metric `azure.compute_vm_scaleset.outbound_flows_maximum_creation_rate.avg` tracks the average rate of outbound network flow creation across a VM scale set. A high value indicates increased network activity, which may signal potential bottlenecks or performance degradation. This could trigger predictive scaling actions to add more VMs, ensuring capacity meets demand and maintaining network efficiency. It directly informs scaling decisions based on network load patterns.
expected_output:
The metric 'azure.compute_vm_scaleset.outbound_flows_maximum_creation_rate.avg' represents the average maximum rate at which new outbound flows (traffic going out of the VM) are being created within a virtual machine scale set. This metric is crucial for understanding the peak demand for outbound network connections from the VMs in the scale set. A high value indicates that there are frequent bursts of outbound traffic, which could suggest that the applications running on these VMs are generating significant network activity, possibly due to data transfers, API calls, or other external communications.

In relation to overall network performance, monitoring this metric helps in identifying potential bottlenecks or congestion in the outbound network paths. It can also indicate whether the current network configuration is adequate to handle the traffic or if upgrades or adjustments are necessary to prevent performance degradation.

Regarding scaling needs, this metric provides insights into the dynamic nature of the workload. If the maximum creation rate of outbound flows is consistently high and shows increasing trends, it may signal that the current number of VM instances in the scale set is insufficient to handle the network demands efficiently. In such cases, scaling up (adding more VMs) or optimizing the existing instances might be required to maintain optimal performance and ensure that the network can handle the expected traffic without delays or failures.

The vSphere integration collects logs and metrics.

Logs help you keep a record of events that happen on your machine. The `Log` data stream collected by vSphere as integration is `log`.

Metrics give you insight into the statistics of the vSphere. The `Metric` data stream collected by the vSphere integration are `cluster`, `datastore`, `datastorecluster`, `host`, `network`, `resourcepool` and `virtualmachine` so that the user can monitor and troubleshoot the performance of the vSphere instance.

Data Streams:

### Virtual Machine

This is the `virtualmachine` data stream. This data stream collects virtual machine metrics from VMware vSphere, including performance statistics such as status, uptime, CPU usage, memory usage, and network activity.

An example event for `virtualmachine` looks as following:

This structure specifies the metrics and target utilization settings for a predictive scaling policy.

You must specify either a metric pair, or a load metric and a scaling metric individually. Specifying a metric pair instead of individual metrics provides a simpler way to configure metrics for a scaling policy. You choose the metric pair, and the policy automatically knows the correct sum and average statistics to use for the load metric and the scaling metric.

input: Given the context provided, how would you determine the significance of 'azure.resource.group' in relation to organizing and managing Azure resources, and what steps would you take to identify all resources within a specific resource group?
actual_output:
The `azure.resource.group` field identifies the resource group in Azure, which organizes resources into logical containers for management, policy application, and access control. To identify all resources in a specific group:  
1. Use Azure CLI/PowerShell: `az resource list --resource-group <group-name>`.  
2. Query logs with `azure.resource.group:<group-name>` to filter resources.  
3. Check `azure.resource.id` entries in logs, which include the group name.  
This helps manage resources efficiently by grouping them under a shared namespace.
expected_output: The 'azure.resource.group' is significant in organizing and managing Azure resources as it serves as a container that holds related resources for an Azure solution. It helps in grouping resources for better management, access control, and cost tracking. To identify all resources within a specific resource group, you would use the 'azure.resource.group' field to filter resources. This can be done through Azure's management tools or APIs where you can specify the resource group name to list all associated resources.

input: Given the context provided, how would you determine the appropriate value for the 'host.id' field if you were setting up monitoring for multiple hosts with potentially identical hostnames across different environments?
actual_output: To determine the appropriate value for `host.id` when hostnames are non-unique, use a unique identifier such as `device_id`, `host_id`, or `provisioningUDID` (depending on the data source). These fields are explicitly mapped to `host.id` in the processors (e.g., `crowdstrike.host.id`, `vectra_detect.log.host.id`, or `jamf_protect.alerts.input.host.provisioningUDID`), ensuring uniqueness across environments.
expected_output: To determine the appropriate value for the 'host.id' field when setting up monitoring for multiple hosts with potentially identical hostnames across different environments, you should use values that are meaningful and unique within your specific environment. According to the context, examples of such values could include the current usage of `beat.name`, which is likely to be unique per host even if hostnames are not. This ensures that each host can be distinctly identified in your monitoring setup, regardless of shared hostnames.

- set:
      field: host.id
      copy_from: vectra_detect.log.host.id
      ignore_empty_value: true
  - rename:
      field: json.edr_type
      target_field: vectra_detect.log.edr_type
      ignore_missing: true
  - append:
      field: related.hosts
      value: '{{{host.hostname}}}'
      allow_duplicates: false
      if: ctx.host?.hostname != null
on_failure:
  - append:
      field: error.message
      value: '{{{_ingest.on_failure_message}}}'
  - set:
      field: event.kind
      value: pipeline_error

---
description: Pipeline for processing Integrity Monitoring event logs
processors:
  - set:
      field: trendmicro.deep_security.event_category
      tag: set_deep_security_event_category
      value: integrity-monitoring-log-event
  - append:
      field: event.category
      tag: set_event_category
      value: configuration
      allow_duplicates: false
  - append:
      field: event.type
      tag: set_event_type
      value: info
      allow_duplicates: false
on_failure:
  - append:
      field: error.message
      value: 'Processor {{{_ingest.on_failure_processor_type}}} with tag {{{_ingest.on_failure_processor_tag}}} in pipeline {{{_ingest.on_failure_pipeline}}} failed with message: {{{_ingest.on_failure_message}}}'
  - set:
      field: event.kind
      value: pipeline_error

allow_duplicates: false
      if: ctx.host?.hostname != null
  - rename:
      field: json.device_id
      tag: rename_device_id
      target_field: crowdstrike.host.id
      ignore_missing: true
  - set:
      field: device.id
      tag: set_device_id_from_host_id
      copy_from: crowdstrike.host.id
      ignore_empty_value: true
  - set:
      field: host.id
      tag: set_host_id_from_host_id
      copy_from: crowdstrike.host.id
      ignore_empty_value: true
  - rename:
      field: json.kernel_version
      tag: rename_kernel_version
      target_field: crowdstrike.host.kernel_version
      ignore_missing: true
  - date:
      field: json.last_seen
      tag: date_last_seen
      target_field: crowdstrike.host.last_seen
      formats:
        - ISO8601
      if: ctx.json?.last_seen != null && ctx.json.last_seen != ''
      on_failure:
        - append:
            field: error.message

- _ingest._value.lastModified
          ignore_missing: true
  - rename:
      field: json.host_groups
      target_field: vectra_detect.log.host.groups
      ignore_missing: true
  - convert:
      field: json.host_id
      tag: 'convert_host_id_to_string'
      target_field: vectra_detect.log.host.id
      type: string
      ignore_missing: true
      if: ctx.json?.host_id != ''
      on_failure:
        - append:
            field: error.message
            value: 'Processor {{{_ingest.on_failure_processor_type}}} with tag {{{_ingest.on_failure_processor_tag}}} in pipeline {{{_ingest.pipeline}}} failed with message: {{{_ingest.on_failure_message}}}'
  - set:
      field: host.id
      copy_from: vectra_detect.log.host.id
      ignore_empty_value: true
  - append:
      field: related.hosts
      value: '{{{host.id}}}'
      allow_duplicates: false
      if: ctx.host?.id != null
  - convert:
      field: json.host_ip
      tag: 'convert_host_ip_to_ip'

#######################
## ECS Host Mapping ##
#######################
    - rename:
        field: jamf_protect.alerts.input.host.hostname
        target_field: host.hostname
        ignore_missing: true
        if: ctx.jamf_protect?.alerts?.input?.host?.hostname != null
    - rename:

        target_field: host.id

    - rename:

    - rename:

input: What is the specific purpose of the `ecs.version` field in the Elastic Common Schema (ECS) and how does it facilitate querying across multiple indices with potentially different ECS versions?
actual_output: The `ecs.version` field specifies the ECS version an event conforms to, ensuring all events include this required metadata. It enables querying across indices with different ECS versions by allowing integrations to adapt to the specific schema version of each event, maintaining compatibility and accurate data interpretation.
expected_output: The `ecs.version` field in the Elastic Common Schema (ECS) specifies the version of ECS that an event conforms to. This field is crucial for ensuring consistency and compatibility when querying across multiple indices that might adhere to slightly different ECS versions. By including `ecs.version`, integrations can dynamically adjust to the schema version of the events they are processing, allowing them to handle variations in field mappings and data structures effectively. This ensures that queries remain accurate and relevant regardless of the ECS version used in different indices.

input: What specific information does the 'host.domain' field provide about the host's membership in a domain, and how does it differ between Windows and Linux environments?
actual_output: The `host.domain` field provides the domain name of which the host is a member. On Windows, this typically refers to the Active Directory domain or NetBIOS domain name. On Linux, it refers to the domain of the host's LDAP provider.
expected_output: The 'host.domain' field provides the name of the domain to which the host belongs. In a Windows environment, this could refer to the host's Active Directory domain or its NetBIOS domain name. On the other hand, in a Linux environment, this field would typically represent the domain associated with the host's LDAP provider.

input: Given the context provided, how would you interpret and explain the meaning of 'azure.resource.tags.*' if you were asked to retrieve all resources tagged with 'environment:production' from an Azure subscription?
actual_output: The provided context does not explicitly mention `azure.resource.tags.*` or tag-based querying. However, in general, `azure.resource.tags.*` would refer to metadata fields in Azure logs that capture resource tags (e.g., `environment:production`). To retrieve such resources, you’d typically use Azure’s resource graph or filter logs based on tag values. The context focuses on event streams and activity logs, not direct tag querying.
expected_output: The field 'azure.resource.tags.*' refers to all tags associated with an Azure resource. To retrieve all resources tagged with 'environment:production', you would query this field for the specific key-value pair where the key is 'environment' and the value is 'production'. This allows you to filter resources based on their tags to find those specifically marked as being in the production environment.

retrieval_context:
# Azure Activity Logs

Azure Activity Logs provide insight into the operations on each Azure resource in the subscription. Use the Activity log to determine the what, who, and when for any write operations taken on the resources in your subscription.

## Requirements and setup

Refer to the [Azure Logs](https://docs.elastic.co/integrations/azure) page for more information about setting up and using this integration.

## Settings

`resource_manager_endpoint` :
_string_
Optional, by default we are using the azure public environment, to override, users can provide a specific resource manager endpoint in order to use a different azure environment.

Resource manager endpoints:

```text
# Azure ChinaCloud
https://management.chinacloudapi.cn/

# Azure GermanCloud
https://management.microsoftazure.de/

# Azure PublicCloud 
https://management.azure.com/

# Azure USGovernmentCloud
https://management.usgovcloudapi.net/
```

## Logs

### activitylogs

The `activitylogs` data stream of the Azure Logs package will collect any activity events that have been streamed through an azure event hub.

input: What specific information does the `ecs.version` field provide in ECS events? List three key points.
actual_output:
1. **Required Field**: Mandatory in all ECS events.  
2. **Schema Compatibility**: Enables integrations to adjust to varying ECS versions across indices.  
3. **Version Identifier**: Specifies the exact ECS version the event conforms to.
expected_output: The `ecs.version` field represents the ECS version that the event conforms to. It is a required field that must exist in all events. This field allows integrations to adjust to the schema version of the events when querying across multiple indices that may conform to slightly different ECS versions.

input: What specific information does 'host.domain' provide about the host's membership in a domain, and how does it differ between Windows and Linux environments?
actual_output: The `host.domain` field provides the domain name of which the host is a member. On **Windows**, this typically refers to the Active Directory domain or NetBIOS domain. On **Linux**, it refers to the LDAP provider's domain. The difference lies in the domain services used by each OS: Windows uses Active Directory/NetBIOS, while Linux uses LDAP.
expected_output: The 'host.domain' field provides the name of the domain to which the host belongs. In a Windows environment, this could refer to the host's Active Directory domain or its NetBIOS domain name. On the other hand, in a Linux environment, this field would typically represent the domain associated with the host's LDAP provider.

input: How does the 'interval' parameter affect the retrieval of metric values, such as 'aws.redshift.metrics.ReadLatency.avg', when querying metrics in Azure Monitor?
actual_output: The 'interval' parameter in the date_histogram operation (e.g., "5m") defines the time bucket granularity for aggregating metric data (like `aws.redshift.metrics.ReadLatency.avg`) during visualization. It does not directly affect the retrieval of raw metric values from Azure Monitor but controls how the data is grouped for display. The actual retrieval resolution depends on the metric's native granularity settings in Azure Monitor.
expected_output: The 'interval' parameter specifies the time interval to use when retrieving metric values in Azure Monitor, formatted as an ISO8601 duration. When querying metrics like 'aws.redshift.metrics.ReadLatency.avg', if the 'interval' is omitted, the metric value is aggregated across the entire timespan of the query. However, if an 'interval' is provided, Azure Monitor may adjust this interval to a more suitable size based on the overall timespan of the query. This allows for more granular or summarized data depending on the specified interval, enabling better analysis of the metric over time.
context:
`interval`:: (_string_) The time interval to use when retrieving metric values. This is an ISO8601 duration.
If interval is omitted, the metric value is aggregated across the entire timespan.
If interval is supplied, the result may adjust the interval to a more appropriate size based on the timespan used for the query.

`aggregation`:: (_[]string_) The aggregation to use when computing the metric values.
To retrieve more than one aggregation at a time, separate them with a comma.
If no aggregation is specified, then the default aggregation for the metric is used.

`segment`:: (_[]string_) The name of the dimension to segment the metric values by.
This dimension must be applicable to the metric you are retrieving.
In this case, the metric data will be segmented in the order the dimensions are listed in the parameter.

`top`:: (_int_) The number of segments to return. This value is only valid when segment is specified.

`aggregations`:: (_[]string_) List of supported aggregations.
Azure Monitor stores all metrics at one-minute granularity intervals. During a given minute, a metric might need to be sampled several times or it might need to be measured for many discrete events.
To limit the number of raw values we have to emit and pay for in Azure Monitor, they will locally pre-aggregate and emit the values:
Minimum: The minimum observed value from all the samples and measurements during the minute.
Maximum: The maximum observed value from all the samples and measurements during the minute.
Sum: The summation of all the observed values from all the samples and measurements during the minute.
Count: The number of samples and measurements taken during the minute.
Total: The total number of all the observed values from all the samples and measurements during the minute.
If no aggregations are filled, the primary aggregation assigned for this metric will be considered.

`name`:: Dimension key
`value`:: Dimension value. (Users can select * to return metric values for each dimension)

`ignore_unsupported`:: (_bool_) Namespaces can be unsupported by some resources and supported in some, this configuration option makes sure no error messages are returned if the namespace is unsupported.
The same will go for the metrics configured, some can be removed from Azure Monitor and it should not affect the state of the module.

Users can select the options to retrieve all metrics from a specific namespace using the following:

Example configuration:

```
    - resource_query: "resourceType eq 'Microsoft.DocumentDb/databaseAccounts'"
      metrics:
      - name: ["DataUsage", "DocumentCount", "DocumentQuota"]
        namespace: "Microsoft.DocumentDb/databaseAccounts"
        ignore_unsupported: true
        dimensions:
        - name: "DatabaseName"
          value: "*"

input: What does azure.dimensions.cloud_role_instance represent in relation to the unique identifier of the cloud instance and how does it differ from cloud.instance.id?
actual_output: The field **azure.dimensions.cloud_role_instance** is not listed in the provided contexts. The unique identifier for a cloud instance is typically **cloud.instance.id**, which directly provides the instance's unique ID. If "cloud_role_instance" refers to a specific role or instance within a cloud environment (e.g., Azure), it may represent a logical grouping or role name rather than a unique ID, differing from **cloud.instance.id** which is a system-assigned unique identifier.
expected_output: azure.dimensions.cloud_role_instance represents the unique identifier of the cloud instance where the application is running, specifically in the context of Azure's cloud services, indicating which particular instance of a cloud role is handling the application. This identifier is used within the Azure ecosystem to distinguish between different instances of the same role. On the other hand, cloud.instance.id is a more general identifier for the host machine in a cloud environment, not specific to Azure's cloud roles. It uniquely identifies the physical or virtual machine instance across different cloud providers, including but not limited to Azure. Therefore, while both identifiers are unique to their respective instances, azure.dimensions.cloud_role_instance is specifically tied to the application's role within Azure, whereas cloud.instance.id is a broader identifier applicable to any cloud instance.

input: What is the specific purpose and use case of the 'geo.name' field in describing user-defined locations?
actual_output: The 'geo.name' field is used to specify the name of a geographical location component, such as a street, city, or region. Its purpose is to provide a human-readable identifier for locations, aiding in address description and mapping. It is typically part of structured address data, enabling precise location referencing in systems like AWS or APIs.
expected_output: The 'geo.name' field is specifically designed to allow users to define a custom description for a location at the granularity that is relevant to them. This could include naming data centers, specifying floor numbers within a building, or even designating city names when these details are more meaningful than generic automated geolocation data. It is not intended for use in automated geolocation processes but rather serves as a way for users to annotate their data with meaningful, human-readable location identifiers.

input: Given the context provided, how would you determine the specific geographic coordinates represented by the 'geo.location' field if you were tasked with plotting these coordinates on a map?
actual_output:
To determine the geographic coordinates from the `geo.location` field for plotting:  
1. Parse the field as a **GeoJSON object** (e.g., `Point`, `Polygon`, or `MultiPolygon`).  
2. Extract the **longitude and latitude** from the `coordinates` array of a `Point` geometry.  
3. For `Polygon`/`MultiPolygon`, retrieve the vertices' coordinates.  
4. Use these coordinates to render the location on a map (e.g., via mapping libraries like Leaflet or GeoPandas).  

This approach leverages GeoJSON standards described in **Context 4**.
expected_output: To determine the specific geographic coordinates represented by the 'geo.location' field for plotting on a map, you would extract the value associated with this field. According to the context, 'geo.location' is described as 'Longitude and latitude.' This means the value stored in 'geo.location' should be a pair of numbers representing the longitude and latitude of a specific location. When plotting, you would use these coordinates directly on a mapping tool or library that supports geo_point data types to place a marker or point at the exact location specified by these coordinates.

input: Given the context provided, how would you determine the significance of 'azure.resource.group' in relation to organizing and managing resources within an Azure environment?
actual_output: The `azure.resource.group` field identifies the Azure resource group to which a resource belongs. This is significant for organizing, managing, and applying policies/access controls across logically grouped resources, enabling efficient governance and monitoring within an Azure environment.
expected_output: The 'azure.resource.group' is significant in organizing and managing resources within an Azure environment as it serves as a container that holds related resources for an Azure solution. It allows users to logically group resources that share the same lifecycle, permissions, and policies, making it easier to manage and deploy them together. By using resource groups, users can apply consistent naming conventions, organize resources hierarchically, and simplify access management through role-based access control (RBAC). This organization helps in efficiently managing large-scale deployments and ensures that resources are properly categorized and accessible.

Conclusion

We know that these are significant vulnerabilities and that customers will want to patch as soon as possible and determine if they were affected by this attack. If you haven’t patched yet (we’ve all been there), hopefully, these searches will provide you the ability to have more visibility into your environment and any malicious activity that you might be experiencing. If they don’t work perfectly, think of them as "SplunkSpiration" :-). As soon as we have more information, we will update this blog. Need Help? Splunk experts are here for you! Customers should reach out to their customer success manager, sales engineer, or regional sales manager.

The detection searches included in this Analytic Story are used to look for suspicious use of WMI commands that attackers may leverage to interact with remote systems. The searches specifically look for the use of WMI to run processes on remote systems.

In the event that unauthorized WMI execution occurs, it will be important for analysts and investigators to determine the context of the event. potential masquerading attempts.

### Possible investigation steps

- Review the process creation event details to identify the full path and name of the executable with a space appended. This can help determine if the file is located in a suspicious or unusual directory.
- Check the process.parent.args field to understand the parent process that initiated the execution. This can provide context on whether the execution was part of a legitimate process chain or potentially malicious activity.
- Investigate the user account associated with the process creation event to determine if the account has a history of executing similar files or if it has been compromised.
- Examine the file's true type and hash to verify its legitimacy and check against known malicious file databases or threat intelligence sources.
- Look for any additional process events or network activity associated with the suspicious executable to identify potential lateral movement or data exfiltration

By default, it will look for the search_name field (seen in index=risk or index=notable) and then look up that value in Splunk Security Essentials to generate the actual techniques, but if you provide a field called mitre_technique (or specify another field name with mitre_technique=myotherfield) it will grab them from that field. input: What does aws.dimensions.Stage represent in the context of AWS API? Please provide a concise explanation and list three examples of how stages might be used.
actual_output:
**aws.dimensions.Stage** in AWS API Gateway represents an environment (e.g., dev, test, prod) for deploying and managing API configurations.  

**Examples of usage:**  
1. Isolating environments (dev, staging, production).  
2. Configuring different logging levels or throttling rates per stage.  
3. Routing traffic to distinct backend services (e.g., test DB vs. production DB).
expected_output:
aws.dimensions.Stage represents a specific version of the API that is accessible to clients. It allows management of different environments or versions of the API, such as development, testing, and production. Examples include:
1. Development Stage - Used for initial development and testing of new features.
2. Testing Stage - Used for rigorous testing before the API goes live.
3. Production Stage - The live version of the API that serves real users.

# AWS API Gateway

The AWS API Gateway integration allows you to monitor [API Gateway](https://aws.amazon.com/api-gateway/) — a centralized API management service.

Use the AWS API Gateway integration to collect metrics and logs related to your HTTP, REST or WebSockets APIs. Then visualize that data in Kibana, create alerts to notify you if something goes wrong, and reference metrics when troubleshooting an issue.

For example, you could use this integration to examine metrics related to error rates, response codes, and latency. You can pinpoint problematic areas, identify error-prone API endpoints, and troubleshoot performance issues.

**IMPORTANT: Extra AWS charges on API requests will be generated by this integration. Check [API Requests](https://www.elastic.co/docs/current/integrations/aws#api-requests) for more details.**

## Data streams

The API Gateway integration collects two types of data: metrics and logs.

# AWS Transit Gateway

The AWS Transit Gateway integration allows you to monitor [AWS Transit Gateway](https://aws.amazon.com/transit-gateway)—a service that connects networks to a single gateway.

Use the AWS Transit Gateway integration collect metrics related to traffic routed between VPCs and on-premises networks. Then visualize that data in Kibana, create alerts to notify you if something goes wrong, and reference metrics when troubleshooting an issue.

For example, you could use this integration to track the number of packets dropped because they did not match a route. Then set up anomaly detection to alert when the number of packets dropped spikes.

**IMPORTANT: Extra AWS charges on API requests will be generated by this integration. Check [API Requests](https://www.elastic.co/docs/current/integrations/aws#api-requests) for more details.**

## Data streams

The AWS Transit Gateway integration collects one type of data: metrics.

input: What does the aws.apigateway.metrics.IntegrationLatency.avg metric represent, and how does it differ from the aws.apigateway.metrics.Latency.avg metric?
actual_output: The `aws.apigateway.metrics.Latency.avg` metric represents the average total latency for API requests, including processing time within API Gateway and waiting for the backend. The `aws.apigateway.metrics.IntegrationLatency.avg` metric specifically measures the average time spent waiting for the backend service to respond, excluding API Gateway's internal processing. The key difference is that `Latency.avg` includes all stages, while `IntegrationLatency.avg` isolates backend wait time.
expected_output:
The aws.apigateway.metrics.IntegrationLatency.avg metric represents the time between when API Gateway relays a request to the backend and when it receives a response from the backend, measured in milliseconds. This specifically measures the latency introduced by the interaction with the backend service.

In contrast, the aws.apigateway.metrics.Latency.avg metric encompasses the entire duration from when API Gateway receives a request from a client until it returns a response to the client. This includes the time taken to process the request, relay it to the backend, receive the response from the backend, and then send the response back to the client. Therefore, the Latency.avg metric provides a broader view of the overall request-response cycle, while the IntegrationLatency.avg focuses solely on the backend interaction time.

input: Given that Docker version 20.10.25 has reached its end-of-life and the ECS-Optimized AL2 AMI is transitioning to Docker 25.0.3, what steps should a user take if they are currently using an ECS-Optimized AL2 AMI released before June 12, 2024, to ensure they receive the updated Docker version and security updates?
actual_output:
To ensure updated Docker and security patches:  
1. **Update in place** with `yum update docker` to manually apply Docker 25.0.3.  
2. **Upgrade to the July 11, 2024 AMI** or later for pre-installed Docker 25.0.3.  
3. **Verify ecs-init version** is 1.81 or later.  
4. **Ensure automatic updates are not disabled** (unless intentional) to receive security updates post-August 28, 2024.
expected_output:
If you are currently using an ECS-Optimized AL2 AMI released before June 12, 2024, to ensure you receive the updated Docker version (25.0.3) and security updates, you should follow these steps:

1. Update your instances manually by running the command 'yum update docker'. This will allow you to perform any necessary acceptance testing with the new Docker version.

2. After successful testing, consider updating to a new version of the ECS-Optimized AL2 AMI released on July 11, 2024 or later. This ensures that your instances are running the latest Docker version and will automatically receive future security updates.

3. If you do not update to the new AMI, you will need to manually manage Docker updates and security patches, as ECS-Optimized AL2 AMIs released between June 12, 2024 and July 11, 2024 will not include Docker 25.0.3 by default.

This will allow customers who update in place, to manually update their instances with the command 'yum update docker' to complete any necessary acceptance testing. We recommend you update to an ECS-Optimized AL2 AMI released on July 11, 2024 or later.

On August 28, 2024, we will tag Docker 25.0.3 as a security update. This will trigger AMIs released prior to June 12, 2024 to automatically update the Docker version to Docker 25.0.3 at instance launch unless you have taken steps to disable this functionality [1].

On August 28, 2024, we will tag Docker 25.0.3 as a security update. This will trigger AMIs released prior to June 12, 2024 to automatically update the Docker version to Docker 25.0.3 at instance launch unless you have taken steps to disable this functionality [1].

Updating in place to Docker 25.0.3 will also update the ecs-init package to the latest available. Docker 25.0.3 is only compatible with ecs-init version 1.81 or later. As a best practice, and to ensure you are receiving the latest package versions and updates, we recommend customers update to the latest Amazon ECS-Optimized AMIs, which are released weekly.

input: When will automatic security updates at instance launch be disabled for ECS-Optimized AL2 AMIs, and what implications does this have for Docker updates?

AMIs released between June 12–July 11, 2024, will not include Docker 25.0.3 by default, necessitating manual updates or AMI upgrades.

input: In the given context, what is the specific purpose and usage of 'aws.cloudwatch.namespace' when querying the CloudWatch API?

This field helps in identifying the group of related metrics that belong to a particular service or application within AWS. When querying the CloudWatch API, you use this namespace to filter and retrieve metrics that are relevant to your specific needs.

---
description: "Pipeline for AWS Billing"
processors:
  - dot_expander:
      field: "*"
      ignore_failure: true
  - set:
      field: cloud.account.name
      copy_from: cloud.account.id
      override: false
      ignore_empty_value: true
  - fingerprint:
      fields: ["aws.billing.group_by"]
      target_field: 'aws.billing.group_by.fingerprint'
      ignore_missing: true
on_failure:
  - set:
      field: event.kind
      value: pipeline_error
  - append:
      field: error.message
      value: >-
        Processor '{{{ _ingest.on_failure_processor_type }}}'
        {{{#_ingest.on_failure_processor_tag}}}with tag '{{{ _ingest.on_failure_processor_tag }}}'
        {{{/_ingest.on_failure_processor_tag}}}failed with message '{{{ _ingest.on_failure_message }}}'

input: What type of data does the AWS CloudFront integration collect, and what are the requirements for setting up this integration?
actual_output:
The AWS CloudFront integration collects **logs** of user requests.  
**Requirements**: AWS Credentials and AWS Permissions for data access.  
Setup may involve configuring S3 bucket ARN or access point ARN if using S3 for log collection.

context:
## Data streams

The AWS CloudFront integration collects one type of data: logs.

**Logs** help you keep a record of every user request that CloudFront receives.
These logs are useful for many scenarios, including security and access audits.
See more details in the [Logs reference](#logs-reference).

## Requirements

Before using any AWS integration you will need:

* **AWS Credentials** to connect with your AWS account.
* **AWS Permissions** to make sure the user you're using to connect has permission to share the relevant data.

For more details about these requirements, please take a look at the [AWS integration documentation](https://docs.elastic.co/integrations/aws#requirements).

## Setup

Use this integration if you only need to collect data from the AWS CloudFront service.

If you want to collect data from two or more AWS services, consider using the
**AWS** integration. When you configure the AWS integration,
you can collect data from as many AWS services as you'd like.

## Data streams

The SQS integration collects one type of data: metrics.

**Metrics** give you insight into the state of AWS SQS.
Metrics collected by the AWS SQS integration include the number of messages that are in flight, the number of ReceiveMessage API calls that did not return a message, and more. See more details in the [Metrics reference](#metrics-reference).

## Requirements

You need Elasticsearch for storing and searching your data and Kibana for visualizing and managing it.
You can use our hosted Elasticsearch Service on Elastic Cloud, which is recommended, or self-manage the Elastic Stack on your own hardware.

Before using any AWS integration you will need:

* **AWS Credentials** to connect with your AWS account.
* **AWS Permissions** to make sure the user you're using to connect has permission to share the relevant data.

For more details about these requirements, please take a look at the [AWS integration documentation](https://docs.elastic.co/integrations/aws#requirements).

## Setup

## Data streams

The AWS VPN integration collects one type of data: metrics.

**Metrics** give you insight into the state of your AWS-hosted VPNs.
Metrics collected by the Amazon VPN integration include the unique host ID, the host IP addresses, the name of the domain of which the host is a member, and more. See more details in the [Metrics reference](#metrics-reference).

## Requirements

You need Elasticsearch for storing and searching your data and Kibana for visualizing and managing it.

For more details about these requirements, please take a look at the [AWS integration documentation](https://docs.elastic.co/integrations/aws#requirements).

# CloudFront

The AWS CloudFront integration allows you to monitor your [AWS CloudFront](https://aws.amazon.com/cloudfront/) usage.

Use the AWS CloudFront integration to collect and parse logs related to content delivery.
Then visualize that data in Kibana, create alerts to notify you if something goes wrong,
and reference logs when troubleshooting an issue.

For example, you could use the data from this integration to know when there are more than some number of failed requests
for a single piece of content in a given time period. You could also use the data to troubleshoot the underlying issue by
looking at additional context in the logs like the number of unique users (by IP address) who experienced the issue,
the source of the request, and more.

**IMPORTANT: Extra AWS charges on API requests will be generated by this integration. Check [API Requests](https://www.elastic.co/docs/current/integrations/aws#api-requests) for more details.**

## Data streams

actual_output: The `aws.cloudfront.time_to_first_byte` field represents the time taken (in seconds) for the first byte of data to be received after a request is made to Amazon CloudFront. It is measured as a floating-point value, converted from the `_tmp.time_to_first_byte` field during data processing.

input: Given that you are collecting log events from multiple log groups using `log_group_name_prefix` and aiming for real-time log ingestion, how would you determine the optimal value for the `number_of_workers` setting based on the number of log groups matched by the prefix, and what potential issues might arise if this setting is not configured correctly?
actual_output: The optimal `number_of_workers` should equal the number of log groups matched by `log_group_name_prefix`. If insufficient, ingestion lags; if excessive, it may overload resources.
expected_output:
To determine the optimal value for the `number_of_workers` setting when collecting log events from multiple log groups using `log_group_name_prefix`, you should count the number of log groups that match the specified prefix. Each log group requires one worker to ensure log ingestion remains as close to real-time as possible. Therefore, the `number_of_workers` should be set equal to the number of matched log groups.

For example, if `log_group_name_prefix` matches five log groups, `number_of_workers` should be set to 5.

If the `number_of_workers` setting is not configured correctly, potential issues may arise:
- If `number_of_workers` is set too low (fewer workers than the number of log groups), some log groups may not be processed in a timely manner, leading to delays in log ingestion and potentially missing recent log entries.
- If `number_of_workers` is set too high (more workers than the number of log groups), resources may be unnecessarily allocated, which could lead to inefficiencies and increased costs without providing any additional benefit to log ingestion performance.
context:
##### Number of workers

If you are collecting log events from multiple log groups using `log_group_name_prefix`, you should review the value of the `number_of_workers`.

The `number_of_workers` setting defines the number of workers assigned to reading from log groups. Each log group matching the `log_group_name_prefix` requires a worker to keep log ingestion as close to real-time as possible. For example, if `log_group_name_prefix` matches five log groups, then `number_of_workers` should be set to `5`. The default value is `1`.

## Logs reference

The `cloudtrail` data stream collects AWS CloudTrail logs. CloudTrail monitors events like
user activity and API usage in AWS services. If a user creates a trail, it delivers those events as log
files to a specific Amazon S3 bucket.

> Note: Use the *CloudTrail Digest Logs regex* setting to define regex to match the path
of the CloudTrail Digest S3 Objects you'd like to read.
If blank, CloudTrail Digest logs will be skipped.

**ECS Field Reference**

##### Number of workers

If you are collecting log events from multiple log groups using `log_group_name_prefix`, you should review the value of the `number_of_workers`.

The `number_of_workers` setting defines the number of workers assigned to reading from log groups. Each log group matching the `log_group_name_prefix` requires a worker to keep log ingestion as close to real-time as possible. For example, if `log_group_name_prefix` matches five log groups, then `number_of_workers` should be set to `5`. The default value is `1`.

## Logs reference

The `ec2` data stream supports both EC2 logs stored in AWS CloudWatch and EC2 logs stored in Amazon S3.
For logs stored in S3, you must export logs from log groups to an Amazon S3 bucket which has SQS notification setup already.

With this data stream, EC2 logs will be parsed into fields like  `ip_address`
and `process.name`. For logs from other services, please use the **AWS CloudWatch** integration.

**ECS Field Reference**

##### Latency

AWS CloudWatch Logs sometimes takes extra time to make the latest logs available to clients like the Agent.

The CloudWatch integration offers the `latency` setting to address this scenario. Latency translates the query's time range to consider the CloudWatch Logs latency. For example, a `5m` latency means the integration will query CloudWatch for logs available 5 minutes ago.

##### Number of workers

If you are collecting log events from multiple log groups using `log_group_name_prefix`, you should review the value of the `number_of_workers`.

The `number_of_workers` setting defines the number of workers assigned to reading from log groups. Each log group matching the `log_group_name_prefix` requires a worker to keep log ingestion as close to real-time as possible. For example, if `log_group_name_prefix` matches five log groups, then `number_of_workers` should be set to `5`. The default value is `1`.

## Logs reference

The `elb` dataset collects logs from AWS ELBs.

**ECS Field Reference**

For example, if `log_group_name_prefix` matches five log groups, then `number_of_workers` should be set to `5`. The default value is `1`.

## Logs reference

The `elb` dataset collects logs from AWS ELBs.

Each log group matching the `log_group_name_prefix` requires a worker to keep log ingestion as close to real-time as possible. For example, if `log_group_name_prefix` matches five log groups, then `number_of_workers` should be set to `5`. The default value is `1`.

input: Given the context provided, how does the 'aws.cloudwatch.namespace' field relate to querying metrics from the CloudWatch API, and what implications might this have on organizing and filtering metric data?
actual_output: The `aws.cloudwatch.namespace` field specifies the CloudWatch namespace used in API queries, categorizing metrics (e.g., "AWS/EC2" for EC2 metrics). It organizes metric data by service or resource type, enabling filtering and analysis. This ensures metrics are grouped logically, simplifying querying, alerting, and dashboard creation by isolating relevant data within specific namespaces.
expected_output: The 'aws.cloudwatch.namespace' field represents the namespace specified when querying the CloudWatch API. This field is crucial for organizing and filtering metric data because it categorizes the metrics based on the service or custom application they belong to. Each AWS service or custom application can define its own set of metrics within a specific namespace. By specifying the correct namespace, users can ensure they are querying the right set of metrics related to their specific needs or services. This organization allows for more precise monitoring, alerting, and analysis of performance and operational data.

Represents a condition to be compared with an attribute value. This condition can be used with DeleteItem, PutItem, or UpdateItem operations; if the comparison evaluates to true, the operation succeeds; if not, the operation fails. You can use ExpectedAttributeValue in one of two different ways:

  • Use AttributeValueList to specify one or more values to compare against an attribute. Use ComparisonOperator to specify how you want to perform the comparison. If the comparison evaluates to true, then the conditional operation succeeds.

DynamoDB cancels a TransactGetItems request under the following circumstances:

  • There is an ongoing TransactGetItems operation that conflicts with a concurrent PutItem, UpdateItem, DeleteItem or TransactWriteItems request. In this case the TransactGetItems operation fails with a TransactionCanceledException.

  • A table in the TransactGetItems request is in a different account or region.

  • There is insufficient provisioned capacity for the transaction to be completed.

  • There is a user error, such as an invalid data format.

# Amazon S3 Storage Lens

The Amazon S3 Storage Lens integration allows you to monitor [Amazon S3 Storage Lens](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/storage-analytics-insights/)—an analytics service for Amazon S3.

Use the Amazon S3 Storage Lens integration to view metrics on object storage usage and activity trends. Then visualize that data in Kibana, create alerts to notify you if something goes wrong, and reference metrics when troubleshooting an issue.

For example, you could track your total storage and object count by region—allowing you more easily visualize trends and anticipate problems before they happen.

**IMPORTANT: Extra AWS charges on API requests will be generated by this integration. Check [API Requests](https://www.elastic.co/docs/current/integrations/aws#api-requests) for more details.**

source:
  index: "logs-*"
  query:
    bool:
      filter:
        - range:
              '@timestamp':
                gte: now-1M
        - exists:
            field: okta.event_type
        - term:
            'data_stream.dataset': "okta.system"
        - term:
            'okta.outcome.result': "SUCCESS"
        - terms:
            'okta.event_type': ["user.session.start","user.session.end"]
dest:
  index: ml_okta_multiple_user_sessions_pad-0.0.1
  aliases:
    - alias: ml_okta_multiple_user_sessions_pad.latest
      move_on_creation: true
    - alias: ml_okta_multiple_user_sessions_pad.all
      move_on_creation: false
description: This transform runs hourly and collects user session information for Okta events for the Privileged Access Detection package.
frequency: 1h
pivot:
  aggregations:
    okta_distinct_ips:
      cardinality:
        field: client.ip
    okta_distinct_countries:
      cardinality:
        field: client.geo.country_name

input: What is the purpose of the `number_of_workers` setting in the context of collecting log events from multiple log groups using `log_group_name_prefix`, and how does it impact real-time log ingestion?
actual_output: The `number_of_workers` setting determines how many workers are assigned to read log groups matching `log_group_name_prefix`. Each log group requires a dedicated worker to maintain real-time log ingestion. If not set appropriately, ingestion may lag. For example, 5 workers are needed for 5 log groups to ensure near real-time processing. The default value is 1.
expected_output: The `number_of_workers` setting is crucial for ensuring efficient and timely log ingestion when collecting log events from multiple log groups using `log_group_name_prefix`. Each log group that matches the specified prefix requires its own worker to handle the log data. By setting `number_of_workers` to match the number of log groups, you ensure that each group is processed concurrently, minimizing delays and keeping log ingestion as close to real-time as possible. This configuration prevents bottlenecks where a single worker might struggle to handle logs from multiple groups simultaneously, thereby maintaining the integrity and timeliness of the collected data.
context:
##### Number of workers

If you are collecting log events from multiple log groups using `log_group_name_prefix`, you should review the value of the `number_of_workers`.

The `number_of_workers` setting defines the number of workers assigned to reading from log groups. Each log group matching the `log_group_name_prefix` requires a worker to keep log ingestion as close to real-time as possible. For example, if `log_group_name_prefix` matches five log groups, then `number_of_workers` should be set to `5`. The default value is `1`.

## Logs reference

The `ec2` data stream supports both EC2 logs stored in AWS CloudWatch and EC2 logs stored in Amazon S3.
For logs stored in S3, you must export logs from log groups to an Amazon S3 bucket which has SQS notification setup already.

With this data stream, EC2 logs will be parsed into fields like  `ip_address`
and `process.name`. For logs from other services, please use the **AWS CloudWatch** integration.

**ECS Field Reference**

##### Number of workers

If you are collecting log events from multiple log groups using `log_group_name_prefix`, you should review the value of the `number_of_workers`.

The `number_of_workers` setting defines the number of workers assigned to reading from log groups. Each log group matching the `log_group_name_prefix` requires a worker to keep log ingestion as close to real-time as possible. For example, if `log_group_name_prefix` matches five log groups, then `number_of_workers` should be set to `5`. The default value is `1`.

## Logs reference

The `cloudtrail` data stream collects AWS CloudTrail logs. CloudTrail monitors events like
user activity and API usage in AWS services. If a user creates a trail, it delivers those events as log
files to a specific Amazon S3 bucket.

> Note: Use the *CloudTrail Digest Logs regex* setting to define regex to match the path
of the CloudTrail Digest S3 Objects you'd like to read.
If blank, CloudTrail Digest logs will be skipped.

**ECS Field Reference**

##### Latency

AWS CloudWatch Logs sometimes takes extra time to make the latest logs available to clients like the Agent.

The CloudWatch integration offers the `latency` setting to address this scenario. Latency translates the query's time range to consider the CloudWatch Logs latency. For example, a `5m` latency means the integration will query CloudWatch for logs available 5 minutes ago.

##### Number of workers

If you are collecting log events from multiple log groups using `log_group_name_prefix`, you should review the value of the `number_of_workers`.

The `number_of_workers` setting defines the number of workers assigned to reading from log groups. Each log group matching the `log_group_name_prefix` requires a worker to keep log ingestion as close to real-time as possible. For example, if `log_group_name_prefix` matches five log groups, then `number_of_workers` should be set to `5`. The default value is `1`.

## Logs reference

The `elb` dataset collects logs from AWS ELBs.

**ECS Field Reference**

monitor network activity.

{{event "firewall_logs" }}

**ECS Field Reference**

Please refer to the following [document](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/ecs/current/ecs-field-reference.html) for detailed information on ECS fields.

{{fields "firewall_logs"}}

## Metrics reference

The `firewall_metrics` dataset collects AWS Network Firewall metrics.

input: Given the context provided, how would you differentiate between aws.ecs.metrics.CPUUtilization.avg and aws.ecs.metrics.CPUReservation.avg, and explain what each metric specifically measures in terms of CPU usage in an AWS ECS cluster?
actual_output:
**aws.ecs.metrics.CPUReservation.avg** measures the percentage of CPU units **reserved** by running tasks in the cluster (allocated capacity).  
**aws.ecs.metrics.CPUUtilization.avg** measures the percentage of CPU units **actually used** by tasks in the cluster (current workload).  

**Key Difference**:  
- **CPUReservation** reflects allocated resources (guaranteed capacity).  
- **CPUUtilization** reflects real-time consumption (actual usage).
expected_output: aws.ecs.metrics.CPUUtilization.avg measures the percentage of CPU units that are actively being used by the tasks running in the cluster or service. In contrast, aws.ecs.metrics.CPUReservation.avg indicates the percentage of CPU units that have been reserved for running tasks in the cluster, regardless of whether they are currently being utilized or not. Essentially, CPUUtilization reflects the actual CPU usage, while CPUReservation shows the allocated CPU capacity.
